did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes SNC_Net, a deep learning-based model that integrates handcrafted and deep learning features for skin cancer detection. It employs a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify eight types of skin cancer using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset, achieving an accuracy of 97.81%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model, SNC_Net, for skin cancer detection. It details the methodology (integrating handcrafted and deep learning features), describes the use of the ISIC 2019 dataset for training and validation, and reports specific performance metrics (97.81% accuracy) compared against baseline and state-of-the-art models. The inclusion of an ablation study and confusion matrix analysis further confirms it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: SNC_Net: Skin Cancer Detection by Integrating Handcrafted and Deep Learning-Based Features Using Dermoscopy Images
brief_summary: This study proposes SNC_Net, a deep learning model that integrates handcrafted and deep learning features to classify eight types of skin cancer using dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The architecture employs a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classification, utilizing Inception V3 for deep feature extraction and handcrafted methods for additional feature representation. To address class imbalance, the SMOTE Tomek approach was applied for sample generation during training. The model's performance was evaluated against four baseline models and six state-of-the-art classifiers using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The proposed SNC_Net achieved an accuracy of 97.81%, precision of 98.31%, recall of 97.89%, and an F1 score of 98.10%, outperforming the compared baseline and state-of-the-art models. The authors conclude that SNC_Net serves as a valuable resource for assisting medical professionals in the early detection of skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Eight distinct types of skin cancer/lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion, Benign Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopy images using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for eight specific skin cancer types. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (97.81%), precision (98.31%), recall (97.89%), and F1 score (98.10%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset for skin cancer detection, comprising images from the MSK Dataset, HAM10000 dataset, and BCN_20000 dataset. The study utilized a subset of this dataset, which was balanced using the SMOTE Tomek technique to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the SNC_Net model after applying SMOTE Tomek for sample generation to balance classes.
use.validation: Used for validating the model (specific split counts not reported, but stated as 'train and validate').
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance against baseline and state-of-the-art classifiers.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the dataset by generating synthetic samples during training, allowing for fair comparison of model performance.

To derive performance parameters including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, true positive rate, true negative rate, false positive rate, and false negative rate for evaluating identification performance.
To compare the discriminatory ability of the proposed SNC_Net model against baseline networks (B1-B4) and to assess performance per class.
To visualize and compare the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate for the proposed method and baseline models.
To validate the performance contribution of specific components (handcrafted vs. deep learning features) and the impact of the SMOTE Tomek technique on the proposed method.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC, loss) and visualizations (confusion matrices, ROC curves, Grad-CAM) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The proposed SNC_Net with SMOTE Tomek outperformed all baseline models and state-of-the-art classifiers in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The ablation study (Table 5) indicates that specific layer configurations (Dropout, Flat Pooling, 2D Pooling) and optimizers (Adam, Nadam) with learning rates of 0.0001 and 0.000001 yielded performance, while other configurations resulted in dropped performance. The paper notes that a more extensive dataset is needed for more accurate results due to class imbalance issues in ISIC 2019.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Integrates handcrafted (HC) feature extraction with deep learning (DL) features
architecture_details.deep_learning_backbone: Inception V3 (used for deep feature extraction)
architecture_details.best_layer_configuration: AveragePooling2D and Dropout layers maintained performance; MaxPooling2D caused performance decline

training_details.hardware: 32 GB RAM, 12 GB NVIDIA GPU, Windows 10

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB dermoscopic images to grayscale
Hair removal using Black-hat (BH) morphological transformation with a 15x15 cross-shaped structural element
Binary thresholding (threshold value < 20) to create a hair removal mask
Image inpainting/painting potential pixels in the neighborhood to remove hair artifacts

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for sample generation to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset. It combines SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for up-sampling minority classes and Tomek Links for under-sampling majority classes, resulting in a balanced dataset with 1600 samples per class (total 8000 samples) for training and validation.

conclusions: The proposed SNC_Net model, which integrates handcrafted and deep learning features using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Inception V3, achieved superior performance in classifying eight types of skin cancer compared to baseline models (EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2, DenseNet-121, ResNet-101) and state-of-the-art classifiers.

SNC_Net with SMOTE Tomek balancing achieved an accuracy of 97.81%, precision of 98.31%, recall of 97.89%, and an F1 score of 98.10%, outperforming the best baseline model (ResNet-101 at 95.80% accuracy) and all compared state-of-the-art methods.

The integration of handcrafted features with deep learning features, combined with the SMOTE Tomek technique for handling class imbalance, significantly improved the model's diagnostic performance, as evidenced by higher AUC values (99.67%) and lower loss values (0.0512) compared to the model without SMOTE.

The study demonstrates that automated detection systems can assist dermatologists and medical professionals in the early and accurate detection of skin cancer, addressing the limitations of manual diagnosis which is labor-intensive and prone to error.

The authors acknowledge that the current dataset (ISIC 2019 subset) has limitations regarding class imbalance and size, suggesting that a more extensive dataset is needed to achieve even more accurate results and validate the model's generalization capabilities.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance; the authors acknowledge that a more extensive and real-world dataset is needed for thorough evaluation.
The model was trained on a balanced subset of ISIC 2019 (1600 samples per class) using SMOTE Tomek, which may not reflect performance on naturally imbalanced clinical data.
The paper explicitly states a limitation: the proposed SNC_Net model was not tested on camera-captured (non-dermoscopy) images, limiting its generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal train/test splits of the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between SNC_Net and baseline models, relying instead on descriptive metrics.

Performance metrics for some state-of-the-art comparisons (e.g., Ref [10], Ref [96]) are incomplete (missing F1 scores), making direct metric-for-metric comparison difficult for those specific baselines.
The hardware environment (32 GB RAM, 12 GB NVIDIA GPU) and software framework (Keras/Python) are specified, but computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) are not reported.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized a subset of the ISIC 2019 dataset, which aggregates images from the MSK Dataset, HAM10000, and BCN_20000. The original subset consisted of 5,750 images with significant class imbalance. To address this, the SMOTE Tomek technique was applied to balance the classes, resulting in a final dataset of 8,000 images (1,600 per class) used for both training and validation. The model was evaluated on this internal split. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper explicitly states that the model was trained and validated on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability, noting that a more extensive, real-world dataset is needed and that the model was not tested on camera-captured (non-dermoscopy) images. No external holdout or cross-dataset experiments were conducted.

training.counts: 8,000 images (1,600 per class after SMOTE Tomek balancing)
training.notes: Used for training the SNC_Net model. The original subset had 5,750 images (450 AKs, 650 BCCa, 850 BK, 250 DFa, 850 MELa, 1550 MNi, 250 SCCa, 250 VASn). SMOTE Tomek was used to generate synthetic samples to balance the classes.

validation.counts: Not explicitly separated from training in counts; part of the 8,000 balanced set
validation.notes: The abstract and methods state the model was trained and validated on the ISIC 2019 dataset. The specific split ratio for validation vs. training is not reported, but the balanced dataset of 8,000 images was used for the experimental setup.

testing.counts: Not explicitly separated from training/validation in counts; part of the 8,000 balanced set
testing.notes: Performance was evaluated on the same ISIC 2019 subset. Confusion matrix details are provided for specific class counts within the test/evaluation phase (e.g., 134 AKs, 147 BCCa, etc.), suggesting a held-out test split from the balanced dataset, though exact split percentages are not reported.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Inception V3 was used for deep feature extraction. While not explicitly stated as ImageNet-pretrained in the text, Inception V3 is a standard ImageNet-pretrained model. The paper focuses on the integration of these deep features with handcrafted features.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: SMOTE Tomek was used to generate synthetic samples for minority classes to balance the dataset. This resulted in 1,600 samples per class for the 8 classes.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Not reported. The paper states the model was trained and validated on the ISIC 2019 dataset. Confusion matrix counts (e.g., 134 AKs, 147 BCCa) suggest a test set was used, but the exact percentage split (e.g., 80/20) is not provided.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: None reported. The study acknowledges the lack of external validation and the need for real-world data.

dataset_level_results.notes: Outperformed baseline models (EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2, DenseNet-121, ResNet-101) and state-of-the-art classifiers. Confusion matrix details provided for specific classes.
Ablation study result showing performance drop without SMOTE Tomek balancing.

generalizability_notes: The study acknowledges that the ISIC 2019 dataset has class imbalance issues, which were addressed via SMOTE Tomek, potentially limiting real-world applicability.
The authors state that a more extensive dataset is needed for more accurate results.
The model was not tested on camera-captured (non-dermoscopy) images, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal train/test splits of the ISIC 2019 dataset.
information_not_reported: Exact percentage split for training, validation, and testing sets.
Specific counts for validation and testing sets (only total balanced count of 8,000 is given, with confusion matrix counts implying a test set).
Patient-level vs. image-level separation details (though image-level is implied by the dataset nature).
Formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for comparisons.
